title: Message Queue — System Design
description: Designing a distributed message queue / log (think Kafka or SQS): producers append messages, consumers read them, and the system guarantees durability, ordering within a partition, and well-defined delivery semantics at high throughput. The interesting problems are the classic distributed-systems ones: partitioning for scale, offsets and consumer groups, at-least-once vs exactly-once effect, ordering, acknowledgement/visibility, replication, and dead-lettering. We build from an in-memory queue toward a partitioned, replicated, consumer-group log.

nodes.label: Ack / Visibility Tracker
nodes.description: Ack / Visibility Tracker: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Broker
nodes.description: Broker: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Broker (leader)
nodes.description: Broker (leader): Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Broker
nodes.description: Broker: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Consumer 1
nodes.description: Consumer 1: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Consumer 2
nodes.description: Consumer 2: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Consumer
nodes.description: Consumer: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Group Coordinator
nodes.description: Group Coordinator: Workflow component that coordinates multi-step operations or compensation.

nodes.label: Dead-Letter Queue
nodes.description: Dead-Letter Queue: Work queue or broker abstraction that buffers tasks for consumers.

nodes.label: Follower
nodes.description: Follower: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Follower Replica
nodes.description: Follower Replica: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Consumer Group
nodes.description: Consumer Group: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Dedup
nodes.description: Dedup: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Dedup Store
nodes.description: Dedup Store: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Leader Replica
nodes.description: Leader Replica: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Append-Only Log
nodes.description: Append-Only Log: Telemetry, monitoring, alerting, logging, dashboard, or audit component.

nodes.label: Consumer Offsets
nodes.description: Consumer Offsets: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Producer
nodes.description: Producer: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Partition 0
nodes.description: Partition 0: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Partition 1
nodes.description: Partition 1: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Partition 2
nodes.description: Partition 2: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Producer
nodes.description: Producer: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Partition Router
nodes.description: Partition Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

functional: Producers publish messages to a named topic.
Consumers subscribe and read messages, tracking their position.
Messages are durable once acknowledged to the producer.
Support multiple independent consumer groups over the same topic.
Dead-letter messages that repeatedly fail processing.
nonFunctional: High throughput: millions of messages per second.
Durability: an acknowledged message is not lost on broker failure.
Ordering within a partition is preserved.
Well-defined delivery semantics (at-least-once by default; exactly-once effect via dedup).
Horizontal scalability by adding partitions and brokers.
Consumers can fail and resume without losing or massively reprocessing messages.

capacity.label: Throughput
capacity.note: Sequential append to a log is the key to high write throughput.

capacity.label: Message size
capacity.note: Many small messages; batching amortizes per-message overhead.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: The log retains messages for a window so consumers can replay.

capacity.label: Consumers
capacity.note: Each group reads independently at its own offset.

capacity.label: Durability
capacity.note: Acked messages survive broker failure via replication.

capacity.label: Ordering scope
capacity.note: Global ordering doesn't scale; ordering is guaranteed within a partition only.

api.description: Publish a message. Optional key decides the partition; producer may batch.
Poll for messages from the partitions assigned to this consumer group member, starting at the committed offset.
Commit the consumer group's offset after processing.

dataModel.name: topics
dataModel.note: A named stream, split into partitions.

dataModel.name: partition_log
dataModel.note: Append-only, ordered sequence of records per partition. Offset is the position.

dataModel.name: consumer_offsets
dataModel.note: Per (group, partition) committed position. The only consumer-side state.

dataModel.name: dedup_ids
dataModel.note: Producer idempotency ids to dedupe retried publishes (exactly-once produce).

patterns.name: Append-only log
patterns.what: Store messages as an immutable, ordered, append-only sequence; position = offset.
patterns.whenToUse: High-throughput durable streams where sequential writes and replay matter.

patterns.name: Partitioning by key
patterns.what: Split a topic into partitions; key hashing keeps a key's messages ordered on one partition.
patterns.whenToUse: Scaling throughput while preserving per-key ordering.

patterns.name: Consumer groups + offsets
patterns.what: Independent groups track their own offset; partitions are balanced across group members.
patterns.whenToUse: Multiple independent readers and horizontal consumer scaling.

patterns.name: At-least-once + idempotency
patterns.what: Redeliver until acked, and dedupe so the effect is exactly-once.

patterns.name: Visibility timeout / ack
patterns.what: Hide an in-flight message until acked or a timeout requeues it.
patterns.whenToUse: Queue-style consumption where a consumer may crash mid-process.

patterns.name: Dead-letter queue
patterns.what: Route repeatedly-failing messages aside instead of blocking or dropping.
patterns.whenToUse: Any retrying consumer with poison messages.

patterns.name: Leader-follower replication
patterns.what: Replicate each partition to followers; ack after replication for durability.
patterns.whenToUse: No-loss durability and availability under broker failure.

steps.title: 1. In-Memory Queue (the baseline)
steps.description: Start with a single broker holding an in-memory FIFO queue: producers push, one consumer pops. It works until the broker restarts (messages lost) or load exceeds one node — which is exactly why the rest of the design exists.
steps.decisionPrompt: What happens to un-consumed messages when the broker process restarts?

concepts.definition: The writer and reader of the queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decoupling them in time is the whole point of a queue.

talkingPoints: Name durability and single-node throughput as the two things this can't do.
Frame the log as the fix for both.

traps.why: A restart loses every un-consumed message.
traps.instead: Persist to a durable, append-only log.

bottlenecks.issue: One node caps throughput and loses data on crash.

strong: Immediately flags durability and single-node limits.
weak: Treats an in-memory queue as sufficient.

whyNow: A baseline makes durability, partitioning, and replication concrete responses to real gaps.

steps.title: 2. Durable Partitioned Log
steps.description: Persist each topic as an append-only log, split into partitions. Sequential appends give high write throughput; a message's offset is its position. A key hashes to a partition so a key's messages stay ordered.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why an append-only log instead of a table with a 'consumed' flag, and how do you keep throughput high?

concepts.definition: A monotonic position of a record within a partition.
concepts.whyItMatters: Consumers track progress by offset; it enables replay.

concepts.definition: Writing to the end of a file/log, not random updates.
concepts.whyItMatters: Sequential I/O is far faster than random — the basis of log throughput.

options.name: Append-only log per partition (default)
pros: Natural retention + replay window; offsets are cheap.
cons: No in-place update/delete; consumers must track offsets.

options.name: Row-per-message table with consumed flag
cons: Random updates to flip flags thrash the DB; doesn't reach log throughput.
Index bloat and vacuuming at high volume.

traps.trap: Using a relational table with a consumed flag as the queue
traps.why: Constant random updates and index churn can't match sequential-append throughput.
traps.instead: Append-only log; consumers advance an offset rather than mutating rows.

traps.trap: Hashing keys to partitions but later changing the partition count
traps.why: Re-hashing scatters a key's history across partitions, breaking per-key ordering.
traps.instead: Treat partition count as semi-fixed; grow with care, or use sticky/explicit partitioning.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Other partitions keep serving; retention reclaims space.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition retention/compaction; alert on disk and lag.

strong: Chooses an append-only log and explains sequential-I/O throughput.
Maps keys to partitions for ordering.
weak: Reaches for a DB table with flags.

recap.after: Durable, ordered, partitioned logs with offsets and high append throughput.
recap.newRisk: Consumers must now track offsets; partition count affects ordering.

whyNow: The log is the core data structure everything else builds on.

flows.name: Produce -> partitioned log -> consumer reads by offset
flows.note: A keyed message is routed to a partition, appended at an offset, and read sequentially by a consumer.

steps.title: 3. Consumer Groups & Offsets
steps.description: Let independent consumer groups read the same topic, each tracking its own committed offset. Within a group, partitions are balanced across members so they scale out; a coordinator reassigns partitions when members join or leave (rebalance).
steps.decisionPrompt: Three consumers in one group read a 3-partition topic. One crashes. What happens?

concepts.definition: A set of consumers that jointly consume a topic, each partition owned by one member.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives parallel consumption with each message processed once per group.
Keeps work distributed and recovers a crashed member's partitions.

deepDives.title: Offset commit timing
points: Commit after processing for at-least-once (a crash before commit reprocesses, not loses).
Commit before processing for at-most-once (a crash loses, never duplicates).
Rebalances briefly pause consumption; keep them rare and fast (static membership, sticky assignment).

traps.trap: Committing the offset before processing the message
traps.why: A crash after commit but before processing loses the message silently.
traps.instead: Commit after successful processing (at-least-once) unless you explicitly want at-most-once.

traps.trap: Storing offsets on the consumer's local disk
traps.why: A replacement instance after a crash has no idea where to resume.
traps.instead: Commit offsets to the broker/durable store keyed by (group, partition).

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Its partitions are reassigned and resume from the last committed offset.
failureDrills.mitigation: Coordinator rebalances; uncommitted messages are redelivered to the new owner.

strong: Explains group ownership, rebalancing, and commit-after-process.
Stores offsets durably keyed by group.

recap.after: Independent groups read in parallel, resuming from durable offsets after failure.
recap.newRisk: Rebalances pause consumption; commit timing decides delivery semantics.

whyNow: Groups + offsets are how consumption scales and survives failure.

steps.title: 4. Delivery Semantics & Idempotency
steps.description: Make the guarantees explicit. The default is at-least-once (redeliver until acked), which means duplicates; achieve exactly-once effect by deduping with producer idempotency ids and idempotent consumers. True exactly-once delivery is impossible, but exactly-once effect is achievable.
steps.decisionPrompt: A producer's publish times out and it retries. How do you avoid a duplicate message in the log?

concepts.definition: At-least-once delivery plus dedup yields a single effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the achievable, useful guarantee — not exactly-once delivery.

concepts.definition: A (producer, sequence) tag the broker uses to drop duplicate appends.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes producer retries safe without duplicating log records.

flows.name: Duplicate publish is deduped
flows.note: Broker drops the retry by idempotency id.

traps.why: It's impossible over an unreliable network; consumers can always crash between act and ack.
traps.instead: Provide at-least-once delivery + dedup to get exactly-once effect.

traps.trap: Non-idempotent consumers on an at-least-once queue
traps.why: Redelivery double-applies side effects (double charge, double email).
traps.instead: Make consumer processing idempotent (dedupe by message id / use upserts).

failureDrills.scenario: Consumer processes a message, then crashes before committing the offset.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The message is reprocessed, not lost; the effect happens once.

recap.after: At-least-once with idempotency gives a clear exactly-once effect.
recap.newRisk: Dedup stores and idempotent consumers are now required.

whyNow: Before relying on the queue for real work, its guarantees must be precise.

steps.title: 5. Acknowledgement, Visibility & Dead-Lettering
steps.description: For queue-style consumption, a polled message becomes invisible for a visibility timeout; the consumer acks on success or it reappears for retry after the timeout. Messages that fail repeatedly go to a dead-letter queue instead of blocking or vanishing.
steps.decisionPrompt: A consumer takes a message but crashes before finishing. How does it get reprocessed without being lost or stuck forever?

concepts.definition: A window during which a delivered message is hidden from other consumers.
concepts.whyItMatters: Prevents two consumers processing the same message while one is still working.

concepts.definition: A side queue for messages that exceed a retry limit.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps poison messages from blocking the queue while preserving them.

traps.trap: Deleting the message as soon as it's delivered
traps.why: If the consumer then crashes, the message is gone — at-most-once by accident.
traps.instead: Hide it for a visibility timeout; delete only on ack, requeue on timeout.

traps.why: It blocks progress and burns capacity.
traps.instead: Cap retries and dead-letter it for inspection.

failureDrills.scenario: A malformed message always throws on processing.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: It stops blocking the queue after a few tries.
failureDrills.mitigation: Retry limit routes it to the DLQ; the rest of the queue flows.

strong: Uses visibility timeout + ack and a DLQ with a retry cap.

recap.before: A crash mid-process could lose or duplicate-block a message.
recap.after: In-flight messages are tracked; failures retry then dead-letter.
recap.newRisk: Visibility timeout tuning and DLQ monitoring are now needed.

whyNow: Queue-style consumers need safe in-flight handling once delivery semantics are set.

flows.name: Visibility timeout & redelivery
flows.note: A polled message is hidden for the visibility timeout; an ack deletes it, otherwise it reappears (and after N failures goes to the DLQ).

else.label: no ack (crash/slow) -> redeliver, then DLQ after N tries

messages.label: after N failures, move to DLQ

steps.title: 6. Replication & Durability
steps.description: Replicate each partition from a leader to followers; the leader acks a publish only after enough followers have the record (in-sync replicas). On leader failure, a follower is promoted. This is what makes 'no loss after ack' true.
steps.decisionPrompt: When exactly do you tell the producer its message is safely stored?

concepts.definition: Followers caught up to the leader; acks wait for a quorum of them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Acking before replication risks loss if the leader then dies.

concepts.definition: Promoting a follower when the leader fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps the partition available without losing acked data.

options.name: Ack after quorum/ISR replication (default)
pros: No loss of acked data if the leader fails.
Tunable durability via the required replica count.
cons: Higher publish latency; availability depends on enough replicas.

options.name: Ack immediately on leader write
cons: A leader crash before replication loses acked messages — violates the durability requirement.

traps.trap: Acking the producer on the leader's local write only
traps.why: If the leader dies before replicating, an 'acked' message is lost.
traps.instead: Ack after a quorum of in-sync replicas have it (tunable).

traps.trap: Electing an out-of-sync follower as leader
traps.why: Promoting a replica missing recent records silently loses acked data.
traps.instead: Only elect from in-sync replicas; refuse otherwise (or accept explicit unclean-election risk).

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: An in-sync follower takes over with no acked data lost.
failureDrills.mitigation: ISR-only leader election; producers retry to the new leader.

strong: Acks after ISR replication and elects only in-sync followers.

recap.before: A broker failure could lose acked messages.
recap.after: Acked messages survive failure via ISR replication and safe failover.
recap.newRisk: Durability costs publish latency and needs enough live replicas.

whyNow: 'No loss after ack' is a core requirement that only replication delivers.

flows.name: Ack after in-sync replication
flows.note: The leader appends, waits for enough followers to replicate, and only then acks the producer (no loss after ack).

steps.title: 7. Scaling Partitions & Brokers
steps.description: Scale throughput by adding partitions and spreading partition leaders across brokers. A router maps keys to partitions; metadata tracks which broker leads each. Adding capacity means rebalancing partitions, done carefully to preserve ordering and minimize movement.
steps.decisionPrompt: One topic's throughput outgrows a single broker. What do you add, and what breaks if you do it naively?

concepts.term: Partition as the unit of parallelism
concepts.definition: More partitions = more parallel producers/consumers and brokers.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the primary scaling lever; consumer parallelism is capped by partition count.
Avoids one broker being the hot leader for everything.

options.name: More partitions, leaders spread across brokers (default)
pros: Scales producer and consumer throughput linearly.
Spreads load; no single hot broker.
cons: Consumer parallelism capped at partition count.
Adding partitions can disturb key->partition mapping/ordering.

options.name: One big partition, scale the broker
pros: Total ordering across the topic; simple.
cons: No parallelism — one consumer at a time; single-node throughput ceiling.

deepDives.title: Repartitioning without chaos
points: Pick partition count generously up front; growing it remaps keys and disturbs per-key ordering.
Spread leaders evenly and move replicas gradually to avoid a rebalance storm.
Consumer parallelism within a group can't exceed partition count — size accordingly.

traps.trap: Requiring total ordering across the whole topic
traps.why: Total order forces a single partition, killing parallelism and throughput.
traps.instead: Scope ordering to a partition (per key); accept no cross-partition order.

traps.trap: Doubling partitions under load with no plan
traps.why: Keys remap, breaking per-key ordering and confusing stateful consumers.
traps.instead: Over-provision partitions early; repartition rarely and deliberately.

strong: Scales via partitions, spreads leaders, scopes ordering per key.
Knows consumer parallelism is bounded by partitions.

recap.before: Throughput was capped by one broker/partition.
recap.after: Partitions and spread leaders scale throughput horizontally.
recap.newRisk: Partition count bounds parallelism and ordering scope; repartitioning is disruptive.

whyNow: Partitioning is the final scaling lever once durability and semantics are solid.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: producers (with idempotency ids) publish to a key-routed, partitioned append-only log; each partition is leader-replicated and acked only after the in-sync set has it; consumer groups read in parallel from durable committed offsets with rebalancing on failure; queue-style consumers use visibility timeouts, acks, and a DLQ; partitions and leaders spread across brokers to scale.

functional.how: Key-routed append to a partitioned log.
Consumer groups with durable committed offsets.
Leader-follower replication; ack after in-sync replicas.
Each group tracks its own offsets over the same log.
Retry cap routes poison messages to a DLQ.

nonFunctional.how: ISR replication and safe leader election.
Key hashing keeps a key's records ordered on one partition.
At-least-once + producer/consumer idempotency for exactly-once effect.
Add partitions and spread leaders across brokers.
Rebalance + resume from committed offsets; visibility timeout requeues in-flight.

say: Restate it: durable, ordered, high-throughput messaging with clear delivery semantics.
List functional (publish, consume, groups, DLQ) and non-functional (throughput, durability, ordering, semantics).
Pin down the guarantees early — they drive everything (at-least-once + idempotency is the usual answer).
Size msgs/s, message size, retention, and number of consumer groups.
Sketch publish, poll, and commit; note the offset is the consumer's only state.
Start in-memory to expose durability, then the append-only partitioned log.
Add consumer groups + offsets, delivery semantics + idempotency, visibility/ack + DLQ, then replication.
Go deep where pushed: exactly-once effect, ISR replication, or repartitioning.
Scale by partitions and leader spread; scope ordering per key.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the dominant tension (ordering vs parallelism, latency vs durability).

expectations: Knows a durable log beats an in-memory queue and that consumers track an offset.
Understands a queue decouples producer and consumer.
Designs partitioning for throughput and per-key ordering, with consumer groups and offsets.
Articulates at-least-once + idempotency for exactly-once effect.
Uses visibility timeouts, acks, and a DLQ.
Specifies ISR replication, ack semantics, and safe leader election for no-loss durability.
Reasons about repartitioning, ordering-vs-parallelism, and rebalance impact.

followUps: How would you implement log compaction to retain only the latest value per key?
How do you support exactly-once across consume->process->produce (transactional messaging)?
How would you handle a slow consumer that falls far behind retention and risks data loss?
How do you do geo-replication across regions and what consistency do you give?
How would you expose backpressure to producers when brokers are saturated?
How do you monitor consumer lag and rebalance storms in production?
